summary: Azure App Service plans, Linux/Windows web apps, and Linux/Windows function apps. Tag updates and plan-tier scaling are self-service; enforcing HTTPS-only is a one-way hardening move that stays guardrailed.

operations.title: Update App Service plan tags
operations.summary: Set or update this plan's tags. Only the tags you list change; others are left alone.
operations.description: Sets or merges tags on the App Service plan — the compute tier shared by every web or function app hosted on it. Non-disruptive, takes effect immediately, no impact on the hosted apps.

params.name: service_plan
params.label: App Service plan
params.help: The App Service plan to tag.

params.name: tags
params.label: Tags
params.help: Key/value tags to set; existing keys not listed here are left untouched. Azure tag names cannot contain < > % & \ ? /.

decisions: Tags are metadata only — cost-neutral and freely reversible — so this stays LOW risk, matching every other tag_update op in the catalog.

operations.title: Resize the App Service plan tier
operations.summary: Change this plan's pricing/compute tier. Every app hosted on the plan shares the new tier immediately.
operations.description: Sets sku_name on the App Service plan, moving it to a different Basic/Standard/PremiumV3 tier. Every web or function app hosted on this plan shares the change immediately; Azure applies plan-tier scaling online without taking the hosted apps offline. Free, Shared, Consumption (Y1), Elastic Premium (EP*), and Isolated (App Service Environment) tiers are out of scope here — they change the plan's architecture rather than just its size, so a move to or from one of them routes to an engineer.

params.name: service_plan
params.label: App Service plan
params.help: The App Service plan to resize.

params.name: new_sku_name
params.label: New tier
params.help: Target Basic/Standard/PremiumV3 tier. Free, Shared, Consumption (Y1), Elastic Premium (EP*), and Isolated tiers change the plan's architecture and are engineer-only.

decisions: Cost-changing — the plan's compute tier drives the App Service bill — so MEDIUM floor.
Azure applies plan-tier scaling online; hosted apps are not taken offline, so downtime stays none.
Free/Shared/Consumption/Elastic Premium/Isolated tiers are excluded from the allowlist — they are architecture changes, not same-shape resizes, and route to an engineer instead.

operations.title: Update Linux web app tags
operations.summary: Set or update this web app's tags. Only the tags you list change; others are left alone.
operations.description: Sets or merges tags on a Linux web app. Non-disruptive, takes effect immediately, no impact on the running app.

params.name: web_app
params.label: Linux web app
params.help: The Linux web app to tag.

params.name: tags
params.label: Tags
params.help: Key/value tags to set; existing keys not listed here are left untouched. Azure tag names cannot contain < > % & \ ? /.

decisions: Tags are metadata only — cost-neutral and freely reversible — so this stays LOW risk.

operations.title: Enforce HTTPS-only on a Linux web app
operations.summary: Reject plain HTTP and require HTTPS for this web app. One-way hardening; loosening it back needs an engineer.
operations.description: Sets https_only to true on a Linux web app, so Azure redirects plain-HTTP requests instead of serving them. Tightening-only — this op can only turn the setting on. Azure applies the change without restarting the app.

params.name: web_app
params.label: Linux web app
params.help: The Linux web app to change.

params.name: https_only
params.label: Require HTTPS
params.help: Set to true to require HTTPS; App Service terminates TLS at the platform, so every app already supports it. This op cannot be used to turn the requirement back off.

decisions: Tighten-only posture — the allowlist offers only true — matching the shared contract's rule for one-way hardening ops.
Security-tightening rather than reachability-opening, so MEDIUM rather than HIGH; loosening back to plain HTTP is not offered here and needs an engineer.
Azure applies the flag without restarting the app, so downtime stays none.

params.name: https_only

operations.title: Create an App Service plan
operations.summary: Request a new App Service plan, the shared compute tier web and function apps run on; an engineer finalizes and reviews the Terraform.
operations.description: Specify a new App Service plan: name, resource group, region, operating system, and pricing/compute tier. Submitting routes it to an engineer with a draft Terraform block to review and finish — nothing is applied without review.

params.name: plan_name
params.label: Plan name
params.help: Letters, numbers and dashes, up to 40 characters. Names the plan in the generated draft.

params.name: resource_group
params.label: Resource group
params.help: The resource group this plan belongs to.

params.name: os_type
params.label: Operating system
params.help: The plan's operating system. Every app hosted on it must match — a Linux web app needs a Linux plan.

params.name: sku_name
params.label: Hosting tier
params.help: Pricing/compute tier — the same list the plan-resize action offers. Every app hosted on the plan shares this tier.

params.name: resource_name
params.label: App Service plan name tag
params.help: Shown as the name everywhere — consoles, tickets, this portal.

params.name: description_tag
params.label: What this App Service plan is for
params.help: One line an operator can act on at 3 AM.

params.name: pic
params.label: Person in charge
params.help: Who to call about this App Service plan.

decisions: Zone balancing and per-site scaling are left at their defaults — enable them explicitly afterward if the workload needs them.
An isolated (App Service Environment) plan is a separate, engineer-authored path — not offered by this form.

operations.title: Create a Linux web app
operations.summary: Request a new Linux web app on an existing App Service plan; an engineer finalizes and reviews the Terraform.
operations.description: Specify a new Linux web app: name, resource group, region, hosting plan, and the baseline site security settings. The application_stack (language/runtime and version, or a container image), custom domains, and app settings are always finished by the engineer, never uploaded here. Submitting routes it to an engineer with a draft Terraform block to review and finish.

params.name: app_name
params.label: Web app name
params.help: Letters, numbers and dashes, 2-60 characters. Becomes part of the app's default <name>.azurewebsites.net address.

params.name: resource_group
params.label: Resource group
params.help: The resource group this web app belongs to.

params.name: service_plan
params.label: App Service plan
params.help: The App Service plan that hosts this app. Must be a Linux plan.

params.name: runtime_stack
params.label: Runtime stack
params.help: The language/runtime and version (or container image) the app runs. Too many valid combinations to offer as a fixed list — the engineer sets the exact block during review.

params.name: https_only
params.label: Require HTTPS
params.help: Always on for a new web app — written into the draft, never asked. Plain HTTP requests are redirected.

params.name: minimum_tls_version
params.label: Minimum TLS version
params.help: Always TLS 1.2 for a new web app — written into the draft, never asked.

params.name: ftps_state
params.label: FTP/FTPS access
params.help: Disabled turns FTP deployment off entirely (the safest default); FtpsOnly allows encrypted FTPS only. Plain unencrypted FTP is never offered.

params.name: resource_name
params.label: Web app name tag
params.help: Shown as the name everywhere — consoles, tickets, this portal.

params.name: description_tag
params.label: What this web app is for
params.help: One line an operator can act on at 3 AM.

params.name: pic
params.label: Person in charge
params.help: Who to call about this web app.

decisions: Confirm the chosen region matches the target App Service plan's own region — Azure requires them to align.
The application_stack (language/runtime and version, or a container image) inside site_config — the engineer sets the exact block during review.
Custom domains, TLS certificate bindings, and deployment slots are configured separately after creation.
App settings and connection strings are deliberately excluded here — they can carry secrets and are added by the engineer after review.

operations.title: Create a Linux function app
operations.summary: Request a new Linux function app on an existing App Service plan; an engineer finalizes and reviews the Terraform.
operations.description: Specify a new Linux function app: name, resource group, region, hosting plan, backing storage account, and the baseline site security settings. The application_stack (language/runtime and version, or a container image), the code package, and app settings are always finished by the engineer, never uploaded here. Submitting routes it to an engineer with a draft Terraform block to review and finish.

params.name: app_name
params.label: Function app name
params.help: Letters, numbers and dashes, 2-60 characters. Becomes part of the app's default <name>.azurewebsites.net address.

params.name: resource_group
params.label: Resource group
params.help: The resource group this function app belongs to.

params.name: service_plan
params.label: App Service plan
params.help: The App Service plan that hosts this app. Must be a Linux plan.

params.name: storage_account
params.label: Storage account
params.help: The storage account the function app uses for its own code and state. Every function app needs one.

params.name: storage_uses_managed_identity
params.label: Use managed identity for storage access
params.help: Always on for a new function app — written into the draft, never asked. Authenticates to the storage account without a shared access key; the engineer still needs to grant the app's managed identity the right role on the storage account.

params.name: runtime_stack
params.label: Runtime stack
params.help: The language/runtime and version (or container image) the app runs. Too many valid combinations to offer as a fixed list — the engineer sets the exact block during review.

params.name: https_only
params.label: Require HTTPS
params.help: Always on for a new function app — written into the draft, never asked. Plain HTTP requests are redirected.

params.name: minimum_tls_version
params.label: Minimum TLS version
params.help: Always TLS 1.2 for a new function app — written into the draft, never asked.

params.name: ftps_state
params.label: FTP/FTPS access
params.help: FTP deployment is turned off entirely for a new function app — written into the draft, never asked.

params.name: resource_name
params.label: Function app name tag
params.help: Shown as the name everywhere — consoles, tickets, this portal.

params.name: description_tag
params.label: What this function app is for
params.help: One line an operator can act on at 3 AM.

params.name: pic
params.label: Person in charge
params.help: Who to call about this function app.

decisions: Confirm the chosen region matches the target App Service plan's own region — Azure requires them to align.

Grant the function app's managed identity the appropriate role (for example Storage Blob Data Owner) on the storage account, and confirm the app carries an identity block — required for storage_uses_managed_identity to actually authenticate.
The application_stack (language/runtime and version, or a container image) inside site_config — the engineer sets the exact block during review.
The code package and its triggers are always delivered by the engineer, never uploaded here.
App settings and connection strings are deliberately excluded here — they can carry secrets and are added by the engineer after review.
